## [00:00 - 03:05]

When we talk about increasing average order value, one of the sites of reference is often Amazon. So Amazon do a great job of working the average order value through the funnel, but not everyone can be Amazon. And what I want you to take away from this in particular is that Amazon has huge brand awareness. People know A around about their kind of availability and range and things like that, but also the pricing as well. So there was actually a study that showed Amazon doesn't always have the best price, but people have been trained on that to the extent that when you have an Amazon login, it's just convenient to just go to Amazon and search and buy the product. Sometimes you can actually save by looking elsewhere, but the other stores miss out because they are not Amazon, right? Amazon are unique in the space in that when you look at their pages and you look at best practice user experience and what you should do for your e-commerce store or your product page or whatever it is, there aren't many e-com stores out there, especially at the kind of lower end. So you're talking about Amazon generating billions or whatever the latest revenue numbers are. For most e-com stores, the sixth figure at the seventh figure at the eighth figure stage, this just wouldn't cut it because for a lot of you, you wouldn't have the brand awareness and the engagement and the stickiness for people to just hang around. Amazon is in effect a membership club. You've got Prime, people just kind of go and kind of leverage Amazon because they have so much, but let's just have a look at Amazon's ability and kind of what they're doing to increase their average order value. Now, I look at something like this and there's different options to potentially increase or kind of work on the average order value. So they literally just offer it all there. They're assuming at this stage, you know what you want. So there's no real explanation of what all these variants are. If it was your e-com store and you're trying to sell to your products, chances are you're trying to sell to your kind of ideal customer, chances are you're going to have to work harder to convince people to upgrade. So whatever your product is, why go for the upgrade? Whereas Amazon don't need to really try. Then as you go through, they've got other methods to try and get you to think about other products as well. Now, for many e-com stores, it'd be fairly suicidal to get, you know, work on your traffic. So let's say, for example, your traffic has been focused on this particular laptop. That's the one that you're selling through Facebook ads. And then you land them on the page and there's so many ways out that would be suicidal for your campaigns because if you've primed someone, especially on Facebook, what I found is when you have a general message on Facebook and say, hey, we sell laptops, including Dell and this and that, and you land them on a category page, you're asking the user to work hard to think about what they want. But now if you're saying that you're advertising this particular Dell laptop that I just randomly

## [03:05 - 06:06]

landed on, you know, it's the new Inspiron 14, blah, blah, blah, blah, you land them on the page, that's all the customers focused on. So if they're in the market, if they're looking to buy this product and you go and send them elsewhere, chances are you're going to lose the sale because they'll be lost in a whole range of different options. And therefore you may just kind of have lost the potential to sell because you've kind of left them with so many different options that they just can't choose. Whereas Amazon don't really have that. So then, you know, you look at the further down the page, they actually do some kind of work on offering you down sales to show you the value of what you're going for. But one of the other values of offering down sales is if that customer comes in and, you know, they wanted the product, a thousand was a bit too much, you're giving them options. So there's a trade off here, the Amazon a plane between converting at the highest revenue number versus just converting. One thing to bear in mind is the profitability on all of these could for all intents and purposes be quite similar. So maybe the margin on this product isn't as good as this product. And you can kind of play around with that as well. And kind of, you know, if you think about your different products is thinking about, yes, your higher ticket item may bring in revenue, but maybe it costs you more as well. And you can actually play around with offering a higher ticket, a higher priced variant of your product, which you don't plan to sell, but you use as a price anchor for the product that you do want to sell. So there was a great example of a newspaper subscription where they offered three tiers and actually the most expensive tier, which had more bundles in it was the same price as the tier down, which was more expensive than the bottom tier. So you kind of look at that and it just messes with your brain that you see two offers there and actually the price the same, but on one of them you're getting so much more. And in your mind, you're thinking, well, I'm obviously going to go for that because it's giving me far more value. So there's different ways that you can set up your products against each other to try and build out that value as well. So then going back to Amazon here, they've got the related items and go down customer zoo, viewed this item also viewed that. And then, you know, as you go through, so if I add this to my basket, I then enter the cross sell journey as well. So here's one here. I can protect my laptop for £172. So now they're bumping up the average order value by offering me insurance. Now in the insurance game, this is highly profitable. Chances are and you know, insurance works on chances.

## [06:06 - 09:07]

Chances are I may not need it. So two year accident or damage, one year breakdown, chance are I would need it. That then becomes profit. So you know, it's something when you think about your products, is thinking about what you can add is a value add on top, but which may turn into profit. I'll give you an example. There was an e-commerce store. We were working on in the jewelry space. And the jewelry came with a really, really nice gift box. Just by default, they offered it. They changed their messaging to say you can get the gift box for an extra $5. That increased their average order value. That increased their profitability because that gift box came with the product anyway. Now this is kind of borderline on ethics of, you know, you were going to give them that anyway. So actually anyone gets that box, but you're actually squeezing out margin. But I'm just giving you ideas of how people are trying to think creatively around average order value. So here, for example, you've got your protection, I'm going to say no thanks there. And then the next page, I'm then offered other items, which people are also purchasing as well. So what they're trying to do at the basket stage is to bump up that value. So you know, one of the interesting things that Amazon also do is they're presenting other laptops. Now, you might look at this and say, well, you bought one laptop. Why would you want another? One thing about Amazon is they test a ton of things. So when you look at this at face value, it might not make sense to you. For Facebook, for Amazon, it probably, there's probably some logic in it. So you know, as you scroll down, there may be a reason as to why this is going on. For you, for example, when you get a customer to add a product into the cart, it's then think about, do I have confidence in offering a cross sell item? So cross sell is if I bought the laptop, maybe it's a laptop stand or an extra battery or storage or things like that. Do I have confidence to offer that product at this stage without the risk of losing the sale? So this is one of the most important things to think about when you're looking to increase your average order value is are you doing it pre-purchase or are you going to do it post-purchase? So you know, if I look at a product funnel, my balance of the decision making is if I've got that customer out of Facebook, they've now clicked on my product page and I'm now trying to convince them to buy this particular product. Am I now thinking about whether the customer is going to convert on this product or whether they're actually going to be distracted? Like you know, when we look at the conversion path, distractions are the biggest challenge

## [09:07 - 10:28]

when it comes to conversion. So if I have a real clean checkout process here and I say, right, you've added to cart, now just go and buy, give us your details, give us your payment details, and we're just going to ship in and that's done. It doesn't mean you can't work on your average order value. You can do it post-purchase. So what that means is, you know, once a customer has placed that order, don't give them the order confirmation page, give them the opportunity or buying an upsell or a cross-sell. So for example, if you're selling the LED light, for example, and you don't want to offer the kind of upsell bundles at the kind of early part of your journey, once they've bought the product, before they get the kind of final thank you page, you could say, hey, just today only, if you buy two or three or four, you can get all these discounts and just click here to add to cart. And you can do that really quickly. And there's different plugins for different platforms out there. But that gives you an idea of how to balance that because this is something you'll need to test. Can you get the average order value up before they make the first purchase? Or can you just push it to the post-purchase experience? And that kind of balance can have a huge impact, not just on your average order value, but also on your conversion rate.
